title: Lesson 1 & 2 – Data Sources and Sampling Techniques
description: Application of sampling methods and data sources used in management information and business analysis
subject: Management Accounting

questions.question: A company wants to test the quality of 20000 invoices generated during the year. It selects 400 invoices for inspection. What is the group of 20000 invoices called?
questions.rationale: Population refers to all items under investigation while the sample is a subset drawn from it.

questions.question: A researcher randomly selects 100 customers from a list of 5000 customers using a random number generator. Which sampling technique is used?
questions.rationale: Simple random sampling gives each population member an equal chance of selection.

questions.question: A supermarket reviews every 10th customer receipt during a selected day to evaluate pricing errors. Which sampling method is being applied?
questions.rationale: Systematic sampling selects every nth item from the population.

questions.question: A bank wants to analyse loan behaviour across income groups. Customers are divided into low income, middle income and high income groups before sampling from each group. Which method is used?
questions.rationale: Stratified sampling divides the population into subgroups and samples from each.

questions.question: Researchers studying shopping behaviour select several cities, then select supermarkets within those cities, and finally survey customers inside those supermarkets. Which method is illustrated?
questions.rationale: Multistage sampling involves selecting samples in stages from progressively smaller subpopulations.

questions.question: Which of the following is the main advantage of sampling instead of conducting a full census?
questions.rationale: Sampling is typically faster and more cost effective than examining the entire population.

questions.question: A researcher divides a city into districts and randomly selects several districts. All households in those districts are surveyed. Which technique is used?
questions.rationale: Cluster sampling selects groups or clusters and studies all elements within them.

questions.question: Which limitation is most associated with systematic sampling?
options: May be biased if patterns exist
Cannot be used for large populations
questions.answer: May be biased if patterns exist
questions.rationale: Systematic sampling may produce bias if the sampling interval coincides with a pattern in the data.

questions.question: Which statement best describes quota sampling?
options: Participants are chosen randomly from each group
Participants are selected until predetermined quotas are reached
questions.answer: Participants are selected until predetermined quotas are reached
questions.rationale: Quota sampling selects individuals based on predefined quotas or characteristics.

questions.question: A company records customer satisfaction ratings such as poor, average, good and excellent. What type of data is this?
questions.rationale: Ordinal data represents categories with a meaningful order or ranking.

questions.question: Which of the following best represents nominal data?
questions.rationale: Nominal data labels categories without ranking or measurement.

questions.question: A company records the number of laptops sold each day. What type of data is this?
questions.rationale: Discrete data represents counted values such as number of items sold.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes continuous data?
questions.rationale: Continuous data is measured and can take any value within a range.

questions.question: A company collects employees' attendance data to calculate salaries. For that purpose the data is considered:
questions.rationale: Primary data is collected directly for the specific purpose it is intended to serve.

questions.question: A business uses previously published government statistics to analyse market trends. What type of data source is this?
questions.rationale: Secondary data refers to data already collected for another purpose.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of transactional data?
questions.rationale: Transactional data records business transactions such as purchases.

questions.question: Temperature readings from a factory machine are an example of which data source?
questions.rationale: Sensor data is generated by devices detecting physical environmental inputs.

questions.question: Which internal system is most likely to provide employee wage data?
questions.rationale: Payroll systems store and process employee salary and wage data.

questions.question: Which of the following is an external source of information for businesses?
questions.rationale: Government data is an external information source outside the organisation.

questions.question: Which advantage is associated with internal sources of information?
options: Readily available and under organisational control
questions.answer: Readily available and under organisational control
questions.rationale: Internal data is easily accessible and controlled by the organisation.

questions.question: Which issue is commonly associated with using external information sources?
questions.rationale: External data may be unreliable or inaccurate because it is outside the organisation’s control.

questions.question: Which of the following are examples of internal sources of information?
questions.rationale: Internal systems such as accounting and payroll are organisational data sources.

questions.question: Which of the following are advantages of sampling?
questions.rationale: Sampling saves time and cost and simplifies data collection but does not eliminate bias.

questions.question: Which of the following are examples of external sources of information?
questions.rationale: External information includes industry associations, internet sources and financial media.

questions.question: A ________ is a group of items drawn from a larger population for analysis.
questions.rationale: A sample represents a subset of the population used for study.

questions.question: Selecting every 8th item from a population list is known as ________ sampling.
questions.rationale: Systematic sampling selects items at regular intervals.

questions.question: Data that can be measured such as time or weight is called ________ data.
questions.rationale: Continuous data is measured and has no gaps in possible values.

questions.question: The [dropdown_1] sampling method gives every member of the population an equal chance of selection, while [dropdown_2] sampling divides the population into subgroups before selecting samples.
questions.rationale: Simple random sampling ensures equal probability while stratified sampling divides populations into strata.

questions.question: Customer purchase records are an example of [dropdown_1] data, while temperature readings from sensors represent [dropdown_2] data.
questions.rationale: Transactional data records business transactions while sensors generate machine data.

questions.question: Government statistics are considered [dropdown_1] sources of information, while payroll records are considered [dropdown_2] sources.
questions.rationale: Government data is external while payroll data originates within the organisation.

questions.question: Match each sampling technique with its description.

matchPairs.right: Every population member has equal chance of selection
Selecting every nth item from a population
Dividing population into subgroups before sampling

questions.question: Match the data type with its example.
Match the data source with its example.
Match the internal system with the information it provides.
Match each external source with the type of information provided.
